item.title: Autonomous Underwater Vehicle - ANAHITA
item.description:
A team of enthusiastic students, professors, and engineers, Team-AUV, works in the area of marine robotics with special interest in automated systems. The team has succesfully manufactured two autonomous underwater vehicles - Varun and Anahita. Both of the vehicles have been taken to national and inetrnational competitions. These machines are capable of navigating in unknown environments, performing acoustic localization, and identifying objects using computer vision. The team has also conducted valuable research in all three - mechanical, electrical, and software systems. The resulting research paper was accepted in IEEE OES Symposium, Porto, now available on arxiv.

I have worked in the team for more than a year in the mechanical subsystem during the development of ANAHITA. We created a light-weight modular frame using abrasive water-jet machining. Since, the entire system works underwater, one of the major mechanical challenges in marine robotics is sealing that we ensured using nitrile O-rings. Several parts and mechanisms had to be meticulously designed for months to ensure precission as well as high scalability. I was mostly involved in grabbing mechanism and design of the main hull. It was an incredibly enriching experience that I suppose one must have if they have the opportunity of being in a technology institute.

item.title: Bayesian Parameter Estimation of 3-Weibull Distribution
item.description:
The problem of parameter estimation for a Weibull distribution is of significant interest in survival analysis. However, many point estimators like MLE, momemts method,and MAP estimates using iterative EM or BFGS methods fail especially when the shape parameter is small and/or the left truncation is high. I (in team of two) worked on studying and implementing Bayesian inference of the unknown parameters of the Weibull distribution.

We followed an approach of factorizing the posterior density of shape and scale parameter into a marginal distribution (of scale parameter) conditioned on data, and a conditional posterior density (of shape parameter) conditioned on the scale parameter and data. We assumed that the posterior distribution for shape parameter is a log-concave function and approximated it to a gamma distribution with unknown hyperparameters. This helped in constructing a Gibbs sampling procedure and obtaining the Bayes estimate of our parameters along with credible intervals.

item.title: Globally centered autocovariances in MCMC
item.description:
Advancements in modern personal computing have made it easy to run parallel Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) implementations. This is particularly useful for slow mixing Markov chains where the starting points of the chains are spread over the state-space in order to more accurately capture characteristics of the target distribution. The output from parallel chains is then summarized, visually and quantitatively, to assess the empirical mixing properties of the chains and the quality of Monte Carlo estimators.

In this project, we proposed a globally-centered autocovariance estimator for multiple-chain MCMC samping that exhibits significant theoretical and empirical improvements. We show that the bias of the G-ACvF estimator is smaller than the bias of the current state-of-the-art. The impact of this improved estimator is evident in three critical output analysis applications: (1) ACF plots, (2) estimates of the Monte Carlo asymptotic covariance matrix, and (3) estimates of the effective sample size. We replace the autocovariance estimator with G-ACvF in SV estimators to obtain a globally-centered SV (G-SV) estimator and demonstrate strong consistency under weak conditions. The large sample bias and variance are also computed and shown to be significantly better.

item.title: Quasi Newton Method for MM Acceleration
item.description:
MM algorithms, along with their abundant statistical applications, suffer from slow convergence, especially when the problem dimension is high. An MM algorithm seeks to optimize some objective function, but can equivalently be expressed without reference to that objective as seeking the fixed points of the algorithm map. There has been some work for accelerating the MM algorithm using quasi-Newton methods. These methods attempt to update the approximation for the Jacobian of the MM algorithm map residual at each iteration. However, most of them are either too problem-specific or rely on certain conditions on starting values or use a scalar approximation for the Jacobian matrix which can cause immense loss of information. We propose a quasi-Newton method for accelerating MM algorithms based on the popular Broyden’s root finding method.

We utilize the information from MM algorithm to construct better secant approximations than standard Broyden’s root finding method and discuss how it acts as a corrective when a classical method jumps to a ‘bad’ state. Borrowing the ideas of convergence from classical Broyden’s literature, we also prove the local and superlinear convergence of our method under reasonable assumptions. Our method while bringing us closer to established wisdom, leads to Jacobians that can be unwieldy in high-dimensional problems. To resolve this problem, we propose a limited memory version of our algorithm that follows the idea limited memory BFGS updates.

item.title: ESS estimation for Importance sampling
item.description:
Effective sample size is an indispensible diagnostic in all Monte Carlo methods. A valid ESS estimator assumes dual responsibilities - 1) it indicates which proposal is best given a family of proposal distributions, and 2) it give a reliable estimate of ESS for given target and proposal distributions. Unfortunately, the ESS estimator for importance sampling has several drawbacks and fails to comply with some of the basic necessities of a valid ESS estimator. However, even in its worst form, it is used often for diagnostics and continues to be the current state-of-the-art.

This is an ongoing projects that aims to develop a bootstrap methodology for estimating variance and effective sample size of importance samples as well as prove their strong consistency. Apart from that we also aim to deveop a sequential stopping criterion for importance sampling that is asymptotically valid. A higher goal for the project is to take these ideas into a multivariate setting wherein calculating ESS is a difficult problem as evident from existing literature.

item.title: Design and Development of Underwater Vehicle: ANAHITA
